preFix.title: Recognising and responding to pneumonia in lambs
preFix.subtitle: The early signs of respiratory disease in young lambs and the steps to take within the first 24 hours.
preFix.excerpt: Pneumonia kills lambs quickly and quietly. A lamb that was standing and feeding at the morning check can be flat by the afternoon. The early signs are subtle: a slight drop in the head, a rough coat, or a cough. Catching them at that stage is what makes the difference between a treatable case and a dead lamb.
preFix.sourceNotes:
DEFRA Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Sheep (2009, Crown copyright). https://www.gov.uk/government/collections/animal-welfare-codes-of-practice
AHDB Beef and Lamb, Pneumonia in Sheep factsheet (2021). https://ahdb.org.uk
SAC Consulting Scotland, Lambing Management Guide. https://www.sruc.ac.uk/sac-consulting

content.text: Pneumonia is the most common infectious cause of lamb death after the first week of life. It usually strikes between one and eight weeks old, with the peak in the second and third weeks. Wet weather, sudden cold snaps. And overcrowded housing create the conditions. The actual disease is usually caused by a mix of viral and bacterial pathogens that are present in most sheep environments.

Lambs that received adequate colostrum in the first six hours of life carry maternal antibodies that help resist the early wave. The lambs that go down first are usually those that were slow to feed. Born as triplets and undersized. Or born to maiden ewes that were slow to let them suck.
Walk the lambing pens twice daily and look at every lamb. The early signs of pneumonia are easy to miss because they look like a lamb that is just slightly off.
Head held lower than usual, not lifted when you enter the pen.
Coat standing rough or staring rather than lying flat.
Not running to suck when the ewe calls.
A single cough or repeated coughing.
Discharge from the nostrils, clear or cloudy.
A lamb showing two or more of these signs needs to come out of the pen immediately for a closer look.

attrs.body: Catch the lamb quietly with a shepherd's crook or by hand and hold it still against your body. Count the breaths for 30 seconds and double the number. A healthy lamb at rest takes 15 to 30 breaths per minute. A rate above 40 at rest. Especially with visible effort in the flanks or the neck stretched out and the mouth slightly open. Is a respiratory rate that needs a vet call.
attrs.title: Checking the respiratory rate

content.text: Catch the lamb with a shepherd's crook and move it into a separate

with its mother. Removing the lamb without the ewe causes distress on both sides and the ewe may reject the lamb when it returns. Keep the pen dry and draught-free; wet bedding in a cold pen makes a sick lamb worse quickly.

Check the respiratory rate once you have the lamb calm. If the rate is above 40 at rest. Or the lamb has visible effort in the flanks, call the vet. Most vets will advise treatment over the phone if you can describe the signs clearly. An antibiotic injection early in the disease gives a much better outcome than waiting.
Managing the environment to reduce spread

Pneumonia spreads between lambs through respiratory droplets in crowded, poorly ventilated housing. If one lamb is affected, others in the same pen are likely already exposed. Reduce stocking density by moving forward any mother-and-lamb pairs that are ready to go out to grass. Improve ventilation at the ridge of the building without creating floor-level draughts.

items.fix: This is a vet call. A lamb that is down, breathing with extended neck, or not standing needs professional assessment and injection treatment.
items.cause: The disease is at an advanced stage, beyond what early observation can catch
items.symptom: Lamb deteriorating despite isolation in a warm pen

items.fix: Move all affected pairs out to a separate area. Increase ventilation. Speak to the vet about a prophylactic treatment programme for the remaining lambs. Review the vaccination status of the ewes before next season.
items.cause: Outbreak of contagious respiratory disease spreading through the lambing shed
items.symptom: Multiple lambs from the same pen coughing within a few days

items.fix: Rub the lamb with the ewe's birth fluid or her own milk to restore the familiar scent. Hold the ewe in a small pen for two to three days with the lamb to force re-bonding.
items.cause: Scent disruption from handling and separation
items.symptom: Ewe repeatedly rejecting the lamb after it returns from treatment pen

content.text: Step-by-step instructions for Recognising and responding to pneumonia in lambs go here.
